content: If the mth term and the nth term of an A.P. are respectively $${1 \over n}$$ and $${1 \over m}$$, then the (mn)th term of the A.P. is
explanation:
Let tm and tn be mth and nth term of an A.P. First term of A.P. is a and common difference is d.

$${t_m} = a + (m - 1)d$$ and $${t_n} = a + (n - 1)d$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over n} = a + (m - 1)d$$ .... (1)

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over m} = a + (n - 1)d$$ .... (2)

Subtracting (2) from (1), we get

$$d(m - 1 - n + 1) = {1 \over n} - {1 \over m} = {{m - n} \over {mn}}$$

$$d(m - n) = {{m - n} \over {mn}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$d = {1 \over {mn}}$$

Substituting value of $$d = {1 \over {mn}}$$ in (1), we get

$$a = {1 \over n} - (m - 1){1 \over {mn}} = {1 \over {mn}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${t_{mn}} = a + (mn - 1)d$$

$$ = {1 \over {mn}} + (mn - 1){1 \over {mn}} = {1 \over {mn}} + 1 - {1 \over {mn}} = 1$$.

content: The 5th term of the series $${{10} \over 9},{1 \over 3}\sqrt {{{20} \over 3}} ,{2 \over 3}$$ is
If a, b, c be in Arithmetic progression, then the value of $$(a + 2b - c)(2b + c - a)(a + 2b + c)$$ is
If three real numbers a, b, c are in Harmonic Progression, then which of the following is true?

options.content: ab, bc, ca are in H.P.
$${a \over b},{b \over c},{c \over a}$$ are in H.P.

explanation:
a, b, c are in H.P.

or, $${1 \over a},{1 \over b},{1 \over c}$$ are in A.P. (reciprocal of H.P. is A.P.)

or, $${{abc} \over a},{{abc} \over b},{{abc} \over c}$$ are in A.P. (multiplying by abc)

or, bc, ac, ab are in A.P.

or, $${1 \over {bc}},{1 \over {ac}},{1 \over {ab}}$$ are in H.P. (reciprocal of A.P. is H.P.)

content: The sum of the infinite series $${\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)^2} + {1 \over 3}{\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)^4} + {1 \over 5}{\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)^6} + ...$$ is
explanation:
$$\because$$ $${\log _e}(1 + x) = x - {{{x^2}} \over 2} + {{{x^3}} \over 3} - {{{x^4}} \over 4} + ...\,|x| < 1$$

$${\log _e}(1 - x) = - x - {{{x^2}} \over 2} - {{{x^3}} \over 3} - {{{x^4}} \over 4} - ...$$

On subtracting

$$lo{g_e}(1 + x) - {\log _e}(1 - x) = 2\left( {x + {{{x^3}} \over 3} + {{{x^5}} \over 5} + ...} \right)$$ ..... (i)

$$\therefore$$ $${\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)^2} + {1 \over 3}{\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)^4} + {1 \over 5}{\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)^6} + ...$$

$$ = {1 \over 3}\left[ {{1 \over 3} + {1 \over 3}{{\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)}^3} + {1 \over 5}{{\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)}^5} + ....} \right]$$

$$ = {1 \over {2 \times 3}}\left[ {{{\log }_e}\left( {1 + {1 \over 3}} \right) - \log \left( {1 - {1 \over 3}} \right)} \right] = {1 \over 6}{\log _e}\left[ {{4 \over 3} \times {3 \over 2}} \right]$$

$$ = {1 \over 6}{\log _e}2$$.

content: If a, b, c are G.P. (a > 1, b > 1, c > 1), then for any real number x (with x > 0, x $$\ne$$ 1) logax, logbx, logcx are in
explanation:
$${b^2} = ac$$ ($$\because$$ a, b, c are in G.P.)

$$ \Rightarrow 2{\log _x}b = {\log _x}ac = {\log _x}a + {\log _x}c$$

(Taking log on base x)

$$ \Rightarrow {2 \over {{{\log }_b}x}} = {1 \over {{{\log }_a}x}} + {1 \over {{{\log }_c}x}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ logax, logbx, logcx are in H.P.

content: If three positive real numbers a, b, c are in A.P. and abc = 4 then the minimum possible value of b is
explanation:
$${{a + c} \over 2} \ge \sqrt {ac} \Rightarrow b \ge \sqrt {{4 \over b}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow {b^{3/2}} \ge 2 \Rightarrow b \ge {2^{2/3}}$$

content: For what value of m, $${{{a^{m + 1}} + {b^{m + 1}}} \over {{a^m} + {b^m}}}$$ is the arithmetic mean of a and b?
explanation:
We know, arithmetic mean of a and b is $${{a + b} \over 2}$$

So $${{{a^{m + 1}} + {b^{m + 1}}} \over {{a^m} + {b^m}}} = {{a + b} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{a^{m + 1}} + 2{b^{m + 1}} = {a^{m + 1}} + {b^{m + 1}} + a{b^m} + {a^m}b$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^{m + 1}} + {b^{m + 1}} = {a^m}b + a{b^m} \Rightarrow {a^{m + 1}} - {a^m}b = a{b^m} - {b^{m + 1}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^m}(a - b) = (a - b){b^m}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^m} = {b^m} \Rightarrow {\left( {{a \over b}} \right)^m} = 1 = {\left( {{a \over b}} \right)^0}$$ $$\because$$ $$a - b \ne 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow m = 0$$

content: The coefficient of xn, where n is any positive integer, in the expansion of (1 + 2x + 3x2 + ....... $$\infty$$)1/2 is
If sum of an infinite geometric series is $${4 \over {3}}$$ and its 1st term is $${3 \over {4}}$$, then its common ratio is
explanation:
Here, $$a = {3 \over 4}$$, $${S_\infty } = {4 \over 3}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${a \over {1 - r}} = {4 \over 2} \Rightarrow 3a = 4 - 4r$$

$$ \Rightarrow {9 \over 4} = 4 - 4r$$

$$\therefore$$ $$r = {7 \over {16}}$$

content: Sum of n terms of the following series 13 + 33 + 53 + 73 ......... is
G.M. and H.M. of two numbers are 10 and 8 respectively. The numbers are
explanation:
Let nos. be a and b

(A = A.M., G = G.M., H = H.M.)

Here, G = 10, H = 8

$$\because$$ G2 = AH $$\Rightarrow$$ 100 = 8A

$$\therefore$$ $$A = {{25} \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ ab = 100 & a + b = 25, which are satisfied by option (a) only.

content: The value of n for which $${{{x^{n + 1}} + {y^{n + 1}}} \over {{x^n} + {y^n}}}$$ is the geometric mean of x and y is
If angles A, B and C are in A.P., then $${{a + c} \over b}$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\because$$ A, B, C are in A.P.

$$\therefore$$ $${{A + C} \over 2} = B$$ ...... (i)

Now, $${{a + c} \over b} = {{2R\sin A + 2R\sin C} \over {2R\sin B}}$$

$$ = {{2\sin \left( {{{A + C} \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {{{A - C} \over 2}} \right)} \over {\sin B}} = 2\cos \left( {{{A - C} \over 2}} \right)$$ [Using (i)]

content: The value of $$\sum\limits_{r = 2}^\infty  {{{1 + 2 + .... + (r - 1)} \over {r!}}} $$
explanation:
We can simplify the numerator of the given series as follows:

$$1 + 2 + .... + (r - 1) = \frac{(r - 1)r}{2}$$

Therefore, the given series becomes:

$$ \sum\limits_{r = 2}^\infty {{{1 + 2 + .... + (r - 1)} \over {r!}}} = \sum\limits_{r = 2}^\infty {{\frac{(r - 1)r}{2} \over {r!}}} = \sum\limits_{r = 2}^\infty {{\frac{(r - 1)r}{2r(r - 1)(r - 2)!}}} = \sum\limits_{r = 2}^\infty {{\frac{1}{2(r - 2)!}}} $$

Now, we can rewrite the series in terms of the exponential series:

$$e^x = \sum\limits_{r = 0}^\infty {{\frac{x^r}{r!}}} = 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^3}{3!} + ... $$

Substituting x = 1 in the above equation, we get:

$$e = \sum\limits_{r = 0}^\infty {{\frac{1}{r!}}} = 1 + 1 + \frac{1}{2!} + \frac{1}{3!} + ... $$

Now, let's manipulate the given series to match the form of the exponential series:

$$ \sum\limits_{r = 2}^\infty {{\frac{1}{2(r - 2)!}}} = \frac{1}{2} \sum\limits_{r = 2}^\infty {{\frac{1}{(r - 2)!}}} = \frac{1}{2} \sum\limits_{r = 0}^\infty {{\frac{1}{r!}}} $$

We can now substitute the value of $$e = \sum\limits_{r = 0}^\infty {{\frac{1}{r!}}} $$ in the above equation to get:

$$ \sum\limits_{r = 2}^\infty {{\frac{1}{2(r - 2)!}}} = \frac{1}{2} \sum\limits_{r = 0}^\infty {{\frac{1}{r!}}} = \frac{e}{2} $$

Therefore, the value of the given series is e/2. So, the correct option is (C).

content: If the sum of ' $n$ ' terms of an A.P. is $3 n^2+5 n$ and its $m$ th term is 164 , then the value of $m$ is
explanation:
First, recall that the $n$th term of an A.P. can be found from the sum of $n$ terms by

$ a_n \;=\; S_n \;-\; S_{n-1}. $

Here

$ S_n \;=\; 3n^2 + 5n, \quad S_{n-1} \;=\; 3(n-1)^2 + 5(n-1). $

Compute $S_{n-1}$:

$ S_{n-1} = 3\bigl(n^2 - 2n +1\bigr) + 5n - 5 = 3n^2 -6n +3 +5n -5 = 3n^2 - n - 2. $

Find $a_n$:

$ a_n = S_n - S_{n-1} = \bigl(3n^2 + 5n\bigr) - \bigl(3n^2 - n - 2\bigr) = 6n + 2. $

We’re given $a_m = 164$. So

$ 6m + 2 = 164 \;\Longrightarrow\; 6m = 162 \;\Longrightarrow\; m = 27. $

Answer: 27 (Option B).

content: The sum of the first four terms of an arithmetic progression is 56 . The sum of the last four terms is 112. If its first term is 11, then the number of terms is
explanation:
Let the first term be $a_1$ and the common difference be $d$.

Given $a_1=11$

The sum of the first four terms is 56 .

$$ \begin{aligned} S_4=4 a_1+\frac{4(4-1)}{2} d & =4 a_1+6 d \\ 4(11)+6 d & =56 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow d=2\\ &\text { The sum of the last four terms can be expressed as }\\ &\begin{aligned} & S_n-S_{n-4} \\ & S_n-S_{n-4}=112 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{n}{2}\left(2 a_1+(n-1) d\right)-\frac{n-4}{2}\left(2 a_1+(n-4-1) d\right)=112 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{n}{2}\left(2(11)+(n-1) 2-\frac{n-4}{2}(2(11)+(n-5) 2)=112\right. \\ & \Rightarrow n(11+n-1)-(n-4)(11+n-5)=112 \\ & \Rightarrow n(n+10)-(n-4)(n+6)=112 \\ & \Rightarrow n^2+10 n-\left(n^2+6 n-4 n-24\right)=112 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad n^2+10 n-\left(n^2+2 n-24\right)=112 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 8 n+24=112 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 8 n=88 \\ & \therefore \quad n=11 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $a, b, c$ are in A.P. and if the equations $(b-c) x^2+(c-a) x+(a-b)=0$ and $2(c+a) x^2+(b+c) x=0$ have a common root, then
explanation:
Given, $a, b, c$ are in AP, so, $2 b=a+c$.

The first equation is $(b-c) x^2+(c-a) x+(a-b)=0$

Substitute $x=1$ into the first equation $(b-c)+(c-a)+(a-b)=0$. Thus, $x=1$ is a root of the first equation.

The second equation is $2(c+a) x^2+(b+c) x=0$

Since, $x=1$ is common root, substitute $x=1$ into the second equation

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2(c+a)+(b+c)=0 \\ & 2 a+2 c+b+c=0 \\ & 2(a+c)+b+c=0 \end{aligned} $$

Substitute $a+c=2 b$;

$$ \begin{aligned} 2(2 b)+b+c & =0 \\ 4 b+b+c & =0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 5 b+c=0 \Rightarrow \quad c & =-5 b \\ \text { Substitute } c=-5 b \text { into } 2 b & =a+c \\ 2 b=a-5 b \Rightarrow a & =7 b \end{aligned} $$

Now, check the options using $a=7 b$ and $c=-5 b$ (B) $a^2, c^2, b^2$ are in A.P.

Check if

$$ \begin{aligned} 2 c^2 & =a^2+b^2 \\ 2(-5 b)^2 & =(7 b)^2+b^2 \\ 2\left(25 b^2\right) & =49 b^2+b^2 \\ 50 b^2 & =50 b^2 \end{aligned} $$

This is true.

$\Rightarrow a^2, c^2, b^2$ are in AP.

content: Given an A.P. and a G.P. with positive terms, with the first and second terms of the progressions being equal. If $$a_n$$ and $$b_n$$ be the $$n^{\text {th }}$$ term of A.P. and G.P. respectively then

If for the series $$a_1, a_2, a_3$$, ...... etc, $$\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{r}}-\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{r}+\mathrm{i}}$$ bears a constant ratio with $$\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{r}+1}$$; then $$\mathrm{a}_1, \mathrm{a}_2, \mathrm{a}_3 \ldots .$$. are in

If $$\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n$$ are in A.P. with common difference $$\theta$$, then the sum of the series $$ \sec \alpha_1 \sec \alpha_2+\sec \alpha_2 \sec \alpha_3+\ldots .+\sec \alpha_{n-1} \sec \alpha_n=k\left(\tan \alpha_n-\tan \alpha_1\right)$$ where $$\mathrm{k}=$$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \alpha_2-\alpha_1=\alpha_3-\alpha_2=\ldots . .=\alpha_n-\alpha_{n-1}=\theta \\\\ & \sec \alpha_1 \sec \alpha_2+\sec \alpha_2 \sec \alpha_3+\ldots .+\sec \alpha_{n-1} \sec \alpha_n \\\\ & =\frac{1}{\cos \alpha_1 \cos \alpha_2}+\frac{1}{\cos \alpha_2 \cos \alpha_3}+\ldots \ldots+\frac{1}{\cos \alpha_{n-1} \cos \alpha_n} \\\\ & =\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\left[\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \alpha_1 \cos \alpha_2}+\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \alpha_2 \cos \alpha_3}+\ldots .+\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \alpha_{n-1} \cos \alpha_n}\right] \\\\ & =\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\left[\frac{\sin \left(\alpha_2-\alpha_1\right)}{\cos \alpha_1 \cos \alpha_2}+\frac{\sin \left(\alpha_3-\alpha_2\right)}{\cos \alpha_2 \cos \alpha_3}+\ldots .+\frac{\sin \left(\alpha_n-\alpha_{n-1}\right)}{\cos \alpha_{n-1} \cos \alpha_n}\right] \\\\ & =\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\left[\left(\tan \alpha_2-\tan \alpha_1\right)+\left(\tan \alpha_3-\tan \alpha_2\right)+\ldots+\left(\tan \alpha_n-\tan \alpha_{n-1}\right)\right] \\\\ & =\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\left[\tan \alpha_n-\tan \alpha_1\right]=\operatorname{cosec} \theta\left(\tan \alpha_n-\tan \alpha_1\right) \\\\ & =k\left(\tan \alpha_n-\tan \alpha_1\right) \\ & \end{aligned} $$

Comparing we get

$$ k=\operatorname{cosec} \theta $$

content: If the n terms $${a_1},{a_2},\,......,\,{a_n}$$ are in A.P. with increment r, then the difference between the mean of their squares & the square of their mean is
explanation:
$$a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$$ are in AP with common difference

$$r>0$$

i.e., $$a_i=a_1+(i-1) r$$

$$=\frac{\sum_\limits{i=1}^n\left[a_1^2+2 a_1 r(i-1)+r^2(i-1)^2\right]}{n}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & n a_1^2+2 a_1 r(0+1+2+\ldots+(n-1)) \\ & =\frac{+r^2\left(0^2+1^2+2^2+. .+(n-1)^2\right)}{n} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

$$=\frac{n a_1^2+2 a_1 r \times \frac{n(n-1)}{2}+r^2 \times \frac{n(n-1)(2 n-1)}{6}}{n}$$

$$=\frac{6 a_1^2+6 a_1 r(n-1)+r^2(n-1)(2 n-1)}{6}$$ ..... (i)

$$\text { Square of mean }=\left(\frac{\sum_\limits{i=1}^n a_i}{n}\right)^2$$

$$=\left[\frac{\frac{n}{2}\left(2 a_1+(n-1) r\right)}{n}\right]^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left(a_1+\frac{(n-1) r}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =a_1^2+r a_1(n-1)+\frac{r^2(n-1)^2}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Required difference

$$=\left[a_1^2+a_1 r(n-1)+\frac{r^2(n-1)(2 n-1)}{6}\right] -\left[a_1^2+r a_1(n-1)+\frac{r^2(n-1)^2}{4}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{r^2(n-1)}{2}\left[\frac{2 n-1}{3}-\frac{n-1}{2}\right] \\ & =\frac{r^2(n-1)}{2}\left(\frac{4 n-2-3 n+3}{6}\right) \\ & =\frac{r^2\left(n^2-1\right)}{12} \end{aligned}$$

$$1, \log _9\left(3^{1-x}+2\right), \log _3\left(4 \cdot 3^x-1\right)$$ are in AP

$$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \log _9\left(3^{1-x}+2\right)=1+\log _3\left(4 \cdot 3^x-1\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{2} \log _3\left(3^{1-x}+2\right)=\log _3 3+\log _3\left(4 \cdot 3^x-1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3^{1-x}+2=3\left(4 \cdot 3^x-1\right) \end{aligned}$$

Let $$\quad 3^x=t$$,

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{3}{t}+2=12 t-3 \\ \Rightarrow & 3+2 t=12 t^2-3 t \\ \Rightarrow & 12 t^2-5 t-3=0 \\ \Rightarrow & t=\frac{5 \pm \sqrt{25+4 \cdot 12 \cdot 3}}{2 \cdot 12}=\frac{5 \pm \sqrt{169}}{24} \\ & =\frac{5 \pm 13}{24}=\frac{-1}{3}, \frac{3}{4} \end{array}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad 3^x=\frac{-1}{3}, \frac{3}{4}\quad$$ (but $$3^x > 0$$)

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 3^x=\frac{3}{4} \\ \Rightarrow & x=\log _3\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)=\log _3 3-\log _3 4 \\ \Rightarrow & x=1-\log _3 4 \end{array}$$

content: Consider a quadratic equation $$a{x^2} + 2bx + c = 0$$ where a, b, c are positive real numbers. If the equation has no real root, then which of the following is true?

options.content: a, b, c cannot be in A.P. or H.P. but can be in G.P.
a, b, c cannot be in G.P. or H.P. but can be in A.P.
a, b, c cannot be in A.P. or G.P. but can be in H.P.
a, b, c cannot be in A.P., G.P. or H.P.

explanation:
We have, a quadratic equation, $$a x^2+2 b x+c=0$$ has no real root.

$$\begin{array}{cc} \Rightarrow & (2 b)^2-4 \times a \times c < 0 \Rightarrow 4 b^2-4 a c < 0 \\ \Rightarrow & 4\left(b^2-a c\right) < 0 \Rightarrow b^2 < a c \\ & b< (a c)^{1 / 2} \end{array}$$

$$b<\mathrm{GM}$$ of $$a, b, c$$

$$\therefore a, b, c$$ are not in GP.

As, we know that $$\mathrm{AM} \geq \mathrm{GM}$$

$$\frac{a+c}{2} \geq(a c)^{1 / 2} \text { and }(a c)^{1 / 2}>b$$

So, $$\quad \frac{a+c}{2}>b$$

$$\therefore a, b \text { and } c \text { are not in AP. }$$

$$\mathrm{HM}=\frac{2 a c}{a+c} \leq \frac{2}{a+c}\left(\frac{a+c}{2}\right)^2 \Rightarrow \frac{2 a c}{a+c} \leq \frac{a+c}{2}$$

But, $$\frac{a+c}{2}>b$$

So, $$\frac{2 a c}{a+c}$$ can be equal to $$b$$.

Hence, a, b and c can't be in AP or GP, but can be in HP.

content: Let $${a_1},{a_2},{a_3},\,...,\,{a_n}$$ be positive real numbers. Then the minimum value of $${{{a_1}} \over {{a_2}}} + {{{a_2}} \over {{a_3}}}\, + \,...\, + \,{{{a_n}} \over {{a_1}}}$$ is
explanation:
Given, numbers are $$a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n$$

We know that $$\mathrm{AM} \geq \mathrm{GM}$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{\frac{a_1}{a_2}+\frac{a_2}{a_3}+\ldots .+\frac{a_n}{a_1}}{n} \geq\left(\frac{a_1}{a_2} \times \frac{a_2}{a_3} \times \ldots \times \frac{a_n}{a_1}\right)^{1 / n}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \frac{\frac{a_1}{a_2}+\frac{a_2}{a_3}+\ldots+\frac{a_n}{a_1}}{n}=(1)^{1 / n} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{a_1}{a_2}+\frac{a_2}{a_3}+\ldots+\frac{a_n}{a_1} \geq n \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \text { Minimum value of } \frac{a_1}{a_2}+\frac{a_2}{a_3}+\ldots+\frac{a_n}{a_1} \text { is } n \text {. }$$

content: If a, b, c are in G.P. and log a $$-$$ log 2b, log 2b $$-$$ log 3c, log 3c $$-$$ log a are in A.P., then a, b, c are the lengths of the sides of a triangle which is
explanation:
By given condition $$2[\log 2b - \log 3c]$$

$$ = \log a - \log 2b + \log 3c - \log a$$

$$\log 3c - \log 2b$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3[\log 2b - \log 3c] = 0$$

$$2b = 3c$$

Also, $${b^2} = ac$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{9{c^2}} \over 4} = ac$$

$$ \Rightarrow c = {{4a} \over 9}$$

$$b = {{3c} \over 2} = {3 \over 2}.{4 \over 9}a = {{2a} \over 3}$$

Since, $$a + b = a + {{2a} \over 3} = {{5a} \over 3} > c$$

$$b + c = {{2a} \over 3} + {{4a} \over 9} = {{10a} \over 9} > a$$

$$c + a = {{13a} \over 9} > b$$

$$\therefore$$ a, b, c are the sides of triangle. Also, as a is the greatest side,

$$\angle$$A of $$\Delta$$ABC

$$\cos A = {{{b^2} + {c^2} - {a^2}} \over {2bc}} = {{{{4{a^2}} \over 9} + {{16{a^2}} \over {81}} - {a^2}} \over {2\,.\,{2 \over 3}a\,.\,{4 \over 9}a}}$$

$$ = {{ - 29} \over {48}} < 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\Delta$$ABC is an obtuse angle.

options.content: $${a_n} < {b_n}$$ if n be even and $${a_n} > {b_n}$$ if n be odd

content: Let a, b, c be real numbers, each greater than 1, such that $${2 \over 3}{\log _b}a + {3 \over 5}{\log _c}b + {5 \over 2}{\log _a}c = 3$$. If the value of b is 9, then the value of 'a' must be
explanation:
Given,

$${2 \over 3}{\log _b}a + {3 \over 5}{\log _c}b + {5 \over 2}{\log _a}c = 3$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{2\log a} \over {3\log b}} + {{3\log b} \over {5\log c}} + {{5\log c} \over {2\log a}} = 3$$

Since, $$A.M = {{{2 \over 3}{{\log }_b}a + {3 \over 5}{{\log }_c}b + {5 \over 2}{{\log }_a}c} \over 3}$$

$$ = {3 \over 3} = 1$$

and $$G.M = {\left( {{2 \over 3}{{\log }_b}a\,.\,{3 \over 5}{{\log }_c}b\,.\,{5 \over 2}{{\log }_a}c} \right)^{{1 \over 3}}}$$

$$ = {2 \over 3}{{\log a} \over {\log b}} \times {3 \over 5}{{\log b} \over {\log c}} \times {5 \over 2}{{\log c} \over {\log a}} = 1$$

$$\therefore$$ $$A.M. = G.M.$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{2\log a} \over {3\log b}} = 1 \Rightarrow {a^2} = {b^3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow a = {({3^6})^{{1 \over 2}}} = {3^3} = 27$$

content: Consider the real valued function h : {0, 1, 2, ...... 100} $$\to$$ R such that h(0) = 5, h(100) = 20 and satisfying h(p) = $${1 \over 2}$$ {h(p + 1) + h(p $$-$$ 1)} for every p = 1, 2 ..... 99. Then the value of h(1) is
The digit in the unit's place of the number 1! + 2! + 3! + .... + 99! is

Three unequal positive numbers a, b, c are such that a, b, c are in G.P. while $$\log \left( {{{5c} \over {2a}}} \right),\log \left( {{{7b} \over {5c}}} \right),\log \left( {{{2a} \over {7b}}} \right)$$ are in A.P. Then a, b, c are the lengths of the sides of
explanation:
Given, a, b, c are in G.P. and $$\log \left( {{{5c} \over {2a}}} \right),\log \left( {{{7b} \over {5c}}} \right),\log \left( {{{2a} \over {7b}}} \right)$$ and A.P.

$$\therefore$$ b2 = ac

and

$$2\log \left( {{{7b} \over {5c}}} \right) = \log \left( {{{5c} \over {2a}}} \right) + \log \left( {{{2a} \over {7b}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{49{b^2}} \over {25{c^2}}} = {{5c} \over {7b}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(7b)^3} = {(5c)^3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 7b = 5c \Rightarrow c = {7 \over 5}b$$

and $${b^2} = ac$$

$$ \Rightarrow {b^2} = a\left( {{7 \over 5}b} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow b = {7 \over 5}a$$

Sides are $${{5b} \over 7},b,{7 \over 5}b$$

$$\therefore$$ a, b, c are the length of the sides of scalene triangle.

content: If a and b are arbitrary positive real numbers, then the least possible value of $${{6a} \over {5b}} + {{10b} \over {3a}}$$ is
explanation:
$${{6a} \over {5b}} + {{10b} \over {3a}}$$

We know that, $$AM \ge GM$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${{{{6a} \over {5b}} + {{10b} \over {3a}}} \over 2} \ge \sqrt {{{6a} \over {5b}} \times {{10b} \over {3a}}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow {{6a} \over {5b}} + {{10b} \over {3a}} \ge 2 \times 2 = 4$$

Hence, least possible value of $${{6a} \over {5b}} + {{10b} \over {3a}}$$ is 4.

content: Let I(n) = n<sup>n</sup>, J(n) = 13.5 ......... (2n $$ - $$ 1) for all (n > 1), n $$ \in $$ N, then
explanation:
Since, AM $$ \ge $$ GM

$$ \therefore $$ $${{1 + 3 + 5 + 7 + ... + (2n - 1)} \over n} > {(J(n))^{{1 \over n}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{n^2}} \over n} > {(J(n))^{{1 \over n}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {n^n} > J(n)$$

$$ \Rightarrow I(n) > J(n)$$

content: Given that n numbers of arithmetic means are inserted between two sets of numbers a, 2b and 2a, b where a, b $$ \in $$ R. Suppose further that the mth means between these sets of numbers are same, then the ratio a : b equals
explanation:
2b = (n + 2)th form

$$ = a + (n + 2 - 1)d$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2b = a + (n + 1)d$$

$$ \Rightarrow d = {{2b - a} \over {n + 1}}$$$$ \therefore $$ m th mean = $$a + m\left( {{{2b - a} \over {n + 1}}} \right)$$

and also, b = (n + 2)th form

$$ = 2a + (n + 2 - 1)d$$

$$ = 2a + (n + 1)d$$

$$ \Rightarrow d = {{b - 2a} \over {n + 1}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ m th mean = $$2a + m\left( {{{b - 2a} \over {n + 1}}} \right)$$

According to the question,

$$a + m\left( {{{2b - a} \over {n + 1}}} \right) = 2a + m\left( {{{b - 2a} \over {n + 1}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a \over b} = {m \over {n + 1 - m}}$$

content: In a GP series consisting of positive terms, each term is equal to the sum of next two terms. Then, the common ratio of this GP series is
explanation:
Let an be the general term of a GP whose first term is a and common ratio is r.

Now according to the question,

$${a_n} = {a_{n + 1}} + {a_{n + 2}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow a{r^{n - 1}} = a{r^n} + a{r^{n + 1}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {r^{n + 1}} = {r^n} + {r^{n + 1}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 = {{{r^n}} \over {{r^{n - 1}}}} + {{{r^{n + 1}}} \over {{r^{n - 1}}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 = r + {r^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {r^2} + r - 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow r = {{ - 1 \pm \sqrt {{{(1)}^2} - 4(1)( - 1)} } \over {2(1)}}$$

$$ = {{ - 1 \pm \sqrt {1 + 4} } \over 2} = {{ - 1 \pm \sqrt 5 } \over 2}$$

Since, GP consists only positive terms

$$ \therefore $$ $$r = {{\sqrt 5 - 1} \over 2}$$

content: If a, x are real numbers and | a | < 1, | x | < 1, then 1 + (1 + a) x + (1 + a + a<sup>2</sup>) x<sup>2</sup> + ..... $$\infty $$ is equal to
The sum of n terms of the following series $${1^3} + {3^3} + {5^3} + {7^3} + ...$$ is
Find the sum of the first n terms of the series 0.2 + 0.22 + 0.222 + ......
The harmonic mean of two numbers is 4. Their arithmetic mean A and the geometric mean G satisfy the relation 2A + G2 = 27. Find the numbers.
explanation:
Let two numbers be x, y.

$$\therefore$$ $${{2xy} \over {x + y}} = 4$$, $$\sqrt {xy} = G$$ & $${{x + y} \over 2} = A$$

$$\therefore$$ $$2{G^2} = 4(2A)$$, $${G^2} = 4A$$

But $$2A + {G^2} = 27$$

$$\therefore$$ $$2A + 4A = 27$$

$$A = {{27} \over 6} \Rightarrow x + y = 9$$

$$\therefore$$ $${G^2} = 18 \Rightarrow xy = 18$$

On solving, we get x = 3, y = 6 or x = 6, y = 3.

content: In a certain test, there are n questions. In this test 2<sup>n-i</sup> students gave wrong answers to at least i questions, where i = 1, 2, ..., n. If the total number of wrong answers given is 2047, then n is equal to
explanation:
Given, total number of questions are n and total wrong answers are 2047

Also, total wrong answer given

$$ = \sum {k \times ({2^{n - k}} - {2^{n(k + 1)}})} $$

$$ \Rightarrow {2^{n - 1}} + ... + 1 = 2047$$

$$ \Rightarrow {2^n} = 2048 = {2^{11}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow n = 11$$

content: Let x<sub>1</sub>, x<sub>2</sub> be the roots of $${x^2} - 3x + a = 0$$ and x<sub>3</sub>, x<sub>4</sub> be the roots of $${x^2} - 12x + b = 0$$. If $${x_1} < {x_2} < {x_3} < {x_4}$$ and $${x_1},{x_2},{x_3},{x_4}$$ are in GP, then ab equals
explanation:
We have,

x1, x2 be the roots of equation $${x^2} - 3x + a = 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${x_1} + {x_2} = 3$$ and $${x_1}{x_2} = a$$

Also, x3, x4 be the roots of equation $${x^2} - 12x + b = 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${x_3} + {x_4} = 12$$ and $${x_3}{x_4} = b$$

Again, x1, x2, x3, x4 are in GP

$$ \therefore $$ $${x_1} = A,{x_2} = AR,{x_3} = A{R^2},{x_4} = A{R^3}$$

Now, $${x_1} + {x_2} = 3$$

$$ \Rightarrow A(1 + R) = 3$$ ....(i)

and $${x_3} + {x_4} = 12$$

$$ \Rightarrow A{R^2}(1 + R) = 12$$ .... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have

$${R^2} = 4 \Rightarrow R = \pm 2$$

When, R = 2, A = 1 and when, R = $$-$$ 2, A = $$-$$ 3

$$ \therefore $$ Numbers are either 1, 2, 4, 8 or $$-$$3, 6, $$-$$12, 24

But $${x_1} < {x_2} < {x_3} < {x_4}$$,

So, $${x_1} = 1,{x_2} = 2,{x_3} = 4,{x_4} = 8$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$ab = {x_1}{x_2}{x_3}{x_4}$$

$$ = 1 \times 2 \times 4 \times 8 = 64$$
